questions.question: State and explain Armstrong's axioms (reflexivity, augmentation, transitivity) used in functional dependency theory.
keyPoints: Reflexivity: If Y âŠ† X, then X â†’ Y
Augmentation: If X â†’ Y, then XZ â†’ YZ
Transitivity: If X â†’ Y and Y â†’ Z, then X â†’ Z
These axioms are sound and complete for deriving all functional dependencies from a given set

questions.question: Define attribute closure with respect to a set of functional dependencies. Compute the closure of {A} for the relation R(A,B,C,D,E) with FDs: Aâ†’BC, CDâ†’E, Bâ†’D, Eâ†’C.
keyPoints: Attribute closure of X (denoted X+) is the set of all attributes functionally determined by X
Iteratively add attributes using given FDs until no more can be added
For A+: Aâ†’BC gives B,C; Bâ†’D gives D; now have A,B,C,D; CDâ†’E gives E; Eâ†’C already present; so A+ = {A,B,C,D,E}

questions.question: Consider relation R(A,B,C,D,E,F) with FDs: Aâ†’BC, Bâ†’E, CDâ†’F, EFâ†’A. (a) Find all candidate keys of R. (b) Compute the canonical cover of the given set of FDs. (c) Using the canonical cover, identify the normal form (1NF/2NF/3NF/BCNF) of R and justify your answer.
keyPoints: Find closure of single attributes and combinations to determine candidate keys: A+ = {A,B,C,E,F} (from Aâ†’BC, Bâ†’E, EFâ†’A gives F), B+ = {B,E}, C+ = {C}, D+ = {D}, E+ = {E}, F+ = {F}; try AD+: Aâ†’BC, Bâ†’E, CDâ†’F, EFâ†’A gives all attributes, so AD is a key; similarly BD, CD, etc.
Canonical cover: Compute F+ to remove redundant FDs and extraneous attributes; F_c = {Aâ†’B, Aâ†’C, Bâ†’E, CDâ†’F, EFâ†’A} after removal
Check for partial dependencies (violates 2NF), transitive dependencies (violates 3NF), and determinant being candidate key (for BCNF)
Aâ†’BC has A as part of key, so not BCNF; 3NF satisfied as Eâ†’B (E is not key but Bâ†’E has B as key? Need careful analysis)

questions.question: Given relation SCHEDULE(CourseID, Professor, Room, Time, StudentID, Grade) with FDs: CourseIDâ†’Professor, Room, Time; (CourseID, Time)â†’Room; (CourseID, Time)â†’Professor; StudentID, CourseIDâ†’Grade. (a) Identify all candidate keys and superkeys. (b) Determine the highest normal form and explain why it does not satisfy higher normal forms. (c) Decompose the relation into 3NF showing the final decomposition.
keyPoints: Candidate keys: StudentID, CourseID (as CourseID determines CourseID, Professor, Room, Time; StudentID, CourseID determines Grade, so StudentID, CourseID is a key); also StudentID, (CourseID, Time) as CourseID, Time determines Room and Professor but not Grade without StudentID
Superkeys: Any superset of candidate keys, e.g., StudentID, CourseID, Time

Highest normal form: 1NF (contains multi-valued attribute? No, but partial dependencies exist: CourseIDâ†’Professor, Room, Time violates 2NF as CourseID is proper subset of key StudentID, CourseID); also transitive dependencies: CourseIDâ†’Professor and Professor is non-prime but determines nothing? Actually CourseIDâ†’Professor, and Professor may determine nothing in this set, so 2NF violation is main issue
Decomposition into 3NF: R1(CourseID, Professor, Room, Time), R2(StudentID, CourseID, Grade), R3(CourseID, Time, Room, Professor) or similar ensuring lossless join and dependency preservation
